item.content:encoded: Big benchmark! Looking back at this article, I'm not terribly pleased with it - Kalinin's criticisms in particular, I've come to see the logic in - but this was one of my first challenge articles, and I'm glad to see it well-received for that reason.

item.title: Re: A thing I did
item.content:encoded: Well, it was originally made for a school project which has been graded. I feel like it's pretty complete as it is.

item.title: Re: A thing I did
item.content:encoded: That's quite cool! Do you plan to keep updating that page?

item.title: A thing I did
item.content:encoded: I made this If I shouldn't have, let me know. I didn't know where to get permission so I just made it. I will take it away if you want. It could be cool though, I think.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The Cult of Amaan Masapi is appealing the late fee. That couldn't possibly be of consequence, no sir.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That last insurance policy. Poor Isabel - she has no idea. Very, very creepy.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Let's not talk about extremes. Of course a bad written GoI is going to suck, and will be downvoted to oblivion.

I'm not going to let you move the goalposts here. You said people voting based on a GoI was "bullshit." Again, that is completely inaccurate.

I'm talking about people who like circus stuff and write about them, and others downvote just because they are tired of clowns, not for the merits of the work.

That's preference and taste. If you don't like that people vote/downvote what they like/don't like, I'm not sure what to say to you beyond, "huh, too bad." Also, you're not really in a position to lecture anyone about voting based on merit.

Yes, they have that right. And I have the right to call bull*t on that.

Yes, you have that right. Having the right to make that statement, however, does not make it any less wrong. You are free to have your own preferences. As is everyone else.

I have seen people downvote because "urrr durr foundation isn't that big durrr" and personally I HATE that.

I'll dispense some free advice here. Adding "hurr durr" to someone's statement is not a very effective form of argument. In fact, all it really does is telegraph the fact that you're not someone who should be taken seriously.

I had a deleted article with some people saying that they downvoted because I used too much commas. So, 3 extra commas in a paragraph is enough reason. I respect if someone doesn’t like the idea, or the execution, but 3 commas…

Without seeing the draft in question, it sounds like you got downvoted because you had incorrect punctuation. You're expected to know how to write correctly here, so yes, that's a valid reason for downvoting. Regardless of your feelings on the matter.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Are you sure people downvoted solely because of "extra commas" and not because it was possibly not written-well?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I understand your point of view, though I disagree, again.

Of course users are free of voting to their heart’s pleasure, they can have many reasons to up or downvote. However, I believe some of them (the ones I pointed out) are bull*t.
Let's not talk about extremes. Of course a bad written GoI is going to suck, and will be downvoted to oblivion. I'm talking about people who like circus stuff and write about them, and others downvote just because they are tired of clowns, not for the merits of the work. Yes, they have that right. And I have the right to call bull*t on that.

The sames applies to headcanon(s). If in my head the Foundation is a small elitist group and write an article with that in mind and do it with sense, then the work has quality. If on the contrary I believe the Foundation is a monster organization and go with that and still get a good one, it has quality too. I have seen people downvote because "urrr durr foundation isn't that big durrr" and personally I HATE that.

About monthly executions, some like it, some don't. I believe it doesn't make sense, too many human beings wasted (and bullets) but still, I won't critique if someone wants to remember that "relic" from the past. It's alright.

And the last one, the couple of effing words. I had a deleted article with some people saying that they downvoted because I used too much commas. So, 3 extra commas in a paragraph is enough reason. I respect if someone doesn’t like the idea, or the execution, but 3 commas…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Now, staff hat off.

As someone whose critique matches a lot of your criteria here, allow me to retort:

  • they like/dont like a GoI.

Users are free to downvote articles based on the inclusion of a particular GoI. Not all groups are created equal, and without naming names (though I'd be happy to do so whenever asked) some groups are more well-constructed from a literary standpoint than others. As a story element that changes the nature of the narrative, this is absolutely fair game for criticism.

  • they like/dont like your headcanon.

Why wouldn't this be a reason to downvote? If someone wrote a piece that relied upon a vision of the Foundation as a collection of 15-year-old Mary Sues with superpowers who did nothing but prank each other all day with the skips, chances are it would be downvoted swiftly, because that's stupid and it makes no sense. Various interpretations of the Foundation engender various reactions in the readership. If people in the readership don't like a particular interpretation, of course they're going to downvote. That's part of overall taste and preference.

  • they like/dont like monthy D executions.

Monthly executions of D-Class makes no logical sense, and is 99% of the time a relic of a different era of the wiki. I can wall of text you on this subject, but it's been done so many times now that I really would rather not unless it's absolutely necessary.

  • they like the skip but not a couple of f***ing words and that's enough to downvote.

…what are you even talking about here? I don't see anything in this thread that even remotely fits that description.

Any member is free to vote any way on an article based on their preference. Your set of preferences will be different than mine or anyone else's. You're straight-up wrong, however, when you call the preferences of others "bullshit." And writers ignore the preferences of the readership at their peril, whether anyone likes that or not.

item.title: STAFF POST - CLOSED
item.content:encoded:
From the Site Rules:

Voting: You may vote for any reason you like, so long as your reasons are based solely on the content of the article.(emphasis mine) Do not downvote/upvote based on your opinion of the author, an effort to manipulate averages, or trying to win contests through attrition, as this is considered malicious voting. Advocating for voting on an article for reasons other than content is strictly against the rules.

I'm going to give you the benefit of the doubt and assume that "i liked the idea, congratz" indicates that you're upvoting the article on its own merits. But you're really close to the line here. I wouldn't test that boundary again.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
upvoted because i disagree with people who downvote skips just because:

  • they like/dont like a GoI.
  • they like/dont like your headcanon.
  • they like/dont like monthy D executions.
  • they like the skip but not a couple of f***ing words and that's enough to downvote.

Tired of that bull***t

BTW, i liked the idea, congratz

item.title: Longwinded Boring Critique
item.content:encoded:
This started off being fairly interesting and slowly became boring. I like the general idea, and unlike a lot of people I kind of like how it interacts with important GoIs and PoIs, but a lot of the interesting stuff is kind of washed out by how far you went with this idea.

I got really fed up with this article when you started talking about the late fees and repossession. This just seems like something tacked on to make this organization seem dangerous. Contrary to common understanding, danger is not essential to a good scip. Objects randomly disappearing is scary, sure, but the general concept doesn't make a whole lot of sense compared to a single repercussion for closure of contract due to late payment, as would more likely happen in real life. That, and inexplicable repossession of worldly goods was done by SCP-142 in pretty much exactly the same way and I'm frankly shocked no one has pointed this out to you yet.

Then you started getting into spatial anomalies in the offices of SCP-2210 and I started to feel like you were just tacking on ideas. I did read the previous comments regarding how this is supposed to fit into the crushing bureaucracy idea by making you perform tasks which may be impossible, in this case visiting an office on the other side of an impossible-to-traverse anomaly, but it still feels like it's added fluff, and by this point in the article a lot of the intrigue and suspension of disbelief starts to drag its heels a bit.

I got really sick of reading about this company after a while and I wanted to hear more about its relationship with the GoIs I was promised, because that's where a lot of the real intrigue lies. Hell, if such a company was made canon, it has the potential to become a GoI, perhaps an anomalous organization which provides anomalous services to certain groups for an inexplicably numerological fee. I was genuinely interested in what this agency does for its patrons, even though it was only mentioned in small chunks toward the end.

The central premise is brought down a bit, though, by the stipulation that they only serve parties interested in "anomalous" things, because at the end of the day what exactly does "anomalous" mean? One individual's space-time anomaly is another's walk to work in the Foundation's universe, and using a word like "anomaly" almost feels like an intentional jab at the central premise. It would be fair to say that only organizations aware of certain anomalies would even know about this company's existence, but to say that they only aid organizations which are keen on such a nebulous concept is quite strange to me. I don't know if anyone else knows what I'm talking about, but imagine an insurance company which solely funds agencies which are interested in the study of the supernatural. It's a self-defeating concept, because anything which these organizations could study would cease to be considered "supernatural" and just become "natural."

Anyway, I'm rambling. This article was kind of ups and downs for me, but I'm going to downvote because while I like having a scip I can bite into this was just too chewy for me and it felt like most of the substance didn't have a lot to it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well, arguably, that's a canon / one interpretation.
I remember Voct asking me to remove the (c) signs after the name when I wrote mine to deliberately leave it open whether it's an actual person or merely a company designation.
While I like the series, I have always somewhat inclined to the second view.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Here's a place to start. (Also the current Wondertainment incarnation is, by general consensus, a woman.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You make a very good point, of course. Rereading it, I actually quite like the answer of Dr. Wondertainment being both a person and a company.

Also, looks like I'm going to have to catch up on my Dr. Wondertainment canon. I had no idea people had started to give him them more of a face.

Edit: Also, shouldn't Marshall, Carter, and Dark have an Ltd. on the end? Or is that just me being pedantic as always?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
While that is technically true, there's been a whole lot of material of late putting a face on Wondertainment, mostly as a way of breaking out from the mold of "thing we don't know what makes things we can't understand".

And anyway, this implies that it is both person and company. The deal has gone down with someone who isn't Wondertainment themselves, but is representing the company, and the deal would look sour to the good Doctor.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: While I quite like this SCP, I would just like to point out that one of the mysteries behind Dr. Wondertainment is that we don't know whether it's a company or a single person behind all of the creations, and this article confirms that there is actually a 'Dr. Wondertainment' creating them. It just kind of irks me, is all.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Oh, that's what you meant.

I personally subscribe to the false termination policy (what danker said).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yes, sorry, I was on my phone and trying to keep it brief, and as a result the wording of my comment was not as clear as I could have been.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
In "Traditional" Foundation Canon, all D-class are executed terminated on a monthly basis.

This is starting to go out of favor, simply because there aren't enough people worldwide who are eligible for the program to keep offing them. Instead, a new common thought is that this is something told to the Foundation Researchers, so they don't feel as guilty about using them in tests that have an extremely high casualty rate.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: In your containment procedures, you mention that D-18272 is exempt from monthly termination, implying that this is something that regularly occurs.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: changed to a no-vote. Still not doing it for me, but the revisions are certainly an improvement.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm not really a fan of the fact that this is an insurance company that doesn't actually sell insurance. I guess that's on purpose, but it makes the company feel kind of off to me from the get-go.

It's explicitly stated in the third paragraph of the description that the company sells 'mundane' insurance policies , although if that is not clear, I will fix that.

This is supposed to be a faceless bureacracy, with all that entails. But what this really is is an organization with the superficial trappings of that theme, without any of what makes such organizations so soul-crushing.

Fair enough. I tried to mix the actual usefulness of an insurance company with Kafkaesque writing, but in retrospect, it is like mixing oil and water. For now, I'm not sure anything can be done about that without completely changing the article.

This further entrenches GoIs on the mainlist. I cannot personally abide such a thing, especially when they're all portrayed on somewhat equal footing. A bunch of magical hipsters and some anomalous carnival barkers have essentially the same business relationship as the owners of the Wanderer's Library. I find these groups all to be inherently silly anyway, and when they interact with the world in ways that don't even make internally consistent sense, it just heightens that. And while we're here, the idea of Wondertainment using human test subjects doesn't really seem consistent with anyone's portrayal of them.

I can understand you dislike the GoI's, but there's nothing I can do about that. I like using GoI's, and so I decided that they would be portrayed here on equal footing because to the insurance company, the group itself does not matter, only the policyholder in the group. As for Wondertainment Industries, I considered that Wondertainment would want children to test his/her toys while they are being designed, but he/she would want real children to do the testing. The rest of the board? Not too concerned about authenticity.

Monthly terminations aren't something that's usually going to be a major basis of my vote, and it isn't here. That said, I have never thought that offing your test subjects en masse on a monthly basis made any sense whatsoever, and references to what is fast becoming a outmoded site trope tend to detract from modern articles, IMO.

The D-class does NOT get offed. Once the payment is made, the D-class enters the Temporal Arresting Pod (which is whatever you want to think a Temporal Arresting Pod is) until the next time the payment has to be made.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Some of the reasons behind why this didn't sit well with me when I initially read it have crystallized.

  • I'm not really a fan of the fact that this is an insurance company that doesn't actually sell insurance. I guess that's on purpose, but it makes the company feel kind of off to me from the get-go.
  • This is supposed to be a faceless bureacracy, with all that entails. But what this really is is an organization with the superficial trappings of that theme, without any of what makes such organizations so soul-crushing. It's got white people in business suits (I'm not sure why they have to be white, but whatever) doing business-y stuff, and if you're late you have to file a lot of nonsensical paperwork in a big labyrinth office. Those are the props of the trope, but what drives the depiction of these kinds of organizations are deeper concepts. Hopelessness. Irrationality. Powerlessness. Depersonalization. Your fate completely in the hands of something you will never be able to understand or penetrate. This organization provides an extremely valuable, understandable service, and it does what it promises as long as you pay on time, for inexplicably cheap amounts. Simply put, you can slap all the paint you want on this, it's not evoking the deeper feelings that I have to think you're going for. Kafka does not approve.
  • This further entrenches GoIs on the mainlist. I cannot personally abide such a thing, especially when they're all portrayed on somewhat equal footing. A bunch of magical hipsters and some anomalous carnival barkers have essentially the same business relationship as the owners of the Wanderer's Library. I find these groups all to be inherently silly anyway, and when they interact with the world in ways that don't even make internally consistent sense, it just heightens that. And while we're here, the idea of Wondertainment using human test subjects doesn't really seem consistent with anyone's portrayal of them.
  • Monthly terminations aren't something that's usually going to be a major basis of my vote, and it isn't here. That said, I have never thought that offing your test subjects en masse on a monthly basis made any sense whatsoever, and references to what is fast becoming a outmoded site trope tend to detract from modern articles, IMO.

Taken together, the above factors make this article just feel kind of…off, which was the feeling that engendered my initial downvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A D-Class is used because as a D-Class, he lacks any true possessions or worth. A low-level researcher has worth and possessions, and even if he is low-level, he may be incapacitated or killed in a containment breach. If the researcher is unable to pay the fee, then the Foundation loses all of its intelligence, plus he loses all of his stuff and gets captured. If the D-Class is unable to pay the fee, he gets captured, but he has no stuff to lose. It's less risk for the Foundation overall.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Neutral for now. I like the concept, but there are some issues I have with it, such as using a D Class for maintaining contact with an anomaly. Why not use a low level researcher since there is no apparent danger?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
On the structure part, if you ever have to deal with a really complicated outdated bureaucracy in person, you'll find yourself doing things like going to one office to get a stack of forms, then going to three others up three flights of stairs and though a maze of hallways to get the information to fill out those forms. You then have to go back to the first office for a signature, at which point you get sent to basement level 5 to deliver a copy to hard records. They then tell you that you also need a signature from 6 different people on floors 2, 4, and 7 before you can file the actual forms. Three of which have a 30 minute waiting line and just went on lunch break. After you get these forms filled out and return to hard records for filing, they ask if you have filled out parts b and c as well, which require the same signatures but different information from 6 other offices hidden somewhere in the building.

And if the building is in the modern architecture style, it will mock you by letting you see the office you need to get to across the atrium with no connecting hallways or staircases.

Its like the scavenger hunt from hell.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Doctor's note removed.

Edited the objects disappearing so that there's slightly less anomalous and slightly more repo reasoning.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
^ Agreed.

Initially I was a little put off by throwing AWCY? around like the more concrete GOIs. I know it's already posted, but I'd like it better if it were intimated that it outputs the contracts according to the Foundation's categorization of GOIs.

And yeah, the doctor's note. The grammar is a little off on the last sentence.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Liked this while it was being written up, and it turned out even better. My only (very minor) complaints are that all products of the Foundation's policy are clearly-linked to known GOIs and that the doctor's note is a bit redundant.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Even though this is a little more meta than I usually go for, I dig this concept a lot.

HOWEVER I chose to downvote because I'm turned off by the seemingly unnecessary spatial paradoxes of the structure. I don't get what they add to the story here, it struck me as superfluous and distracting.

Also, physical disappearance of objects/people to collect fees. The mysterious utility insurance fees were a very nice explanation for the collection, but then it jumps to YOUR STUFF VANISHES INTO THIN AIR. The stark contrast there upended my suspension of disbelief. It seems like a very lazy explanation.

Normally I would just no-vote where I like the concept but not so much the execution. I'm downvoting because the above were particularly distracting to me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Victims of the full process have been observed working in SCP-2210, albeit in an unaltered condition.

Should this say "altered" instead of "unaltered?"

I really like the idea of an anomalous insurance company, but I'm not as keen on an insurance company that goes beyond insurance in so many broad ways. I also feel the bureaucracy of forms and corridors is a bit trite; that bit, though fairly well-written, was long-winded and I knew exactly where it was going.

Neutral vote.

item.title: Fixed the XXXXs
item.content:encoded:
Posted in response to the Bright Challenge.

Thanks to everyone who critiqued this, and to InspidParoxysm for the original idea.

See more of my work on my author page!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: XXXX's everywhere
